hints: The table has a unique center point.
Play first at the exact center, then mirror every opponent move through the center.
You always have a reply ⇒ go first (answer 1).
solution: Place the first quarter exactly at the center, then mirror each opponent placement through the center. Every move they can make has a symmetric reply, so you never run out first — you should go first, answer $1.$
With Jack $=n$ and Jill $=100-n$: $\tfrac14(100-n)-\tfrac13 n=11$, i.e. $25-\tfrac{7n}{12}=11$, so $\tfrac{7n}{12}=14$ and $n=24.$

hints: Bet bᵢ so payouts equalize: 2b₁=4b₂=6b₃=R, with b₁+b₂+b₃=1.
solution: Choose bets so every outcome returns $R$: $b_1=\tfrac R2,b_2=\tfrac R4,b_3=\tfrac R6$. Then $R\big(\tfrac12+\tfrac14+\tfrac16\big)=\tfrac{11}{12}R=1$, so $R=\tfrac{12}{11}$ and the guaranteed profit is $R-1=\tfrac1{11}.$

hints: With k pairs left (2k cards), a draw removes a pair w.p. 1/(2k−1).
So removing one pair takes a geometric mean of 2k−1 turns.
solution: When $k$ pairs remain ($2k$ cards), a random draw of two matches with probability $\dfrac{k}{\binom{2k}{2}}=\dfrac1{2k-1}$, so clearing that pair takes on average $2k-1$ turns. Summing, $\mathbb{E}[T]=\sum_{k=1}^{10}(2k-1)=100.$
Doubling the cost equation and subtracting the count equation gives $99x_1+19x_2=1900$. Reducing mod $99$, $x_2=1$, so $x_1=\tfrac{1881}{99}=19$ and $x_3=80$. Thus $x_1x_2x_3=19\cdot1\cdot80=1520.$

hints: Threshold strategy: keep all cards ≥ k; f(k)=(100+k)/2 − (k−1).
f(k)=51 − k/2 is decreasing in k.
solution: Keeping all cards $\ge k$ pays the mean $\tfrac{100+k}{2}$ minus the $k-1$ discard costs: $f(k)=51-\tfrac k2$, decreasing in $k$. So keep everything ($k=1$), giving $f(1)=\tfrac{101}{2}.$

hints: Payoff = 2·1(S≥24) − 1; replicate with 2 binary calls at K=24 minus 1 bond.
solution: The payoff is $2\cdot\mathbf 1(S_T\ge24)-1$, replicated by $2$ binary calls struck at $24$ minus one bond. The price is $2(0.73)-0.9=1.46-0.9=0.56.$

hints: Minimize the year first; months need a leading 0 or 1 and days need small digits.
Years 20XX consume 0 and 2, blocking valid all-distinct month/day combos.
The first workable year is 2345 (digits 2,3,4,5), with 06/17 → 06/17/2345.
solution: All-distinct dates require the four year digits plus a valid month and day with no repeats. Years $20\text{XX}$ tie up $0$ and $2$, leaving no room for a legal month/day, and the same obstruction rules out everything up to $2344$. The first feasible year is $2345$ (digits $2,3,4,5$), and the earliest month/day from the remaining $\{0,1,6,7,8,9\}$ is $06/17$. So the date is $06/17/2345$, i.e. $06172345.$

hints: With n sixes and 18−n threes, EV=(6n+3(18−n))/18.
